title: Why Have Inflation Expectations Surged Recently? A Historical Perspective
summary: Federal Reserve Bank of Boston researchers Philippe Andrade and Michael Wicklein analyze household inflation expectations using University of Michigan Survey of Consumers data and a regression model linking expectations to salient prices (gasoline, food) and core CPI. They find that the March 2025 surge in inflation expectations—like the late-1970s surge—cannot be fully explained by actual price increases, unlike the early 1970s and pandemic-era spikes. This unexplained component suggests increased risk of de-anchoring of inflation expectations, which could fuel additional price pressures and complicate the Fed's path to its 2 percent inflation target.
key_points: One-year-ahead average household inflation expectations have risen above 8 percent in four episodes since 1960: 1973-1975, 1978-1980, 2021-2022, and March 2025 onward.
The early 1970s and pandemic-era surges can be largely explained by steep increases in gas and food prices and broad-based inflation.
The late-1970s surge and the March 2025 surge were not as closely related to price increases, suggesting an autonomous or de-anchoring component.
Researchers used CPI for gasoline and food as measures of salient prices, combined with core CPI to model household inflation expectations.
The authors cite prior research showing household inflation expectations are strongly influenced by changes in gasoline prices (Coibion and Gorodnichenko 2015) and food prices (D'Acunto et al. 2020).

Near-term household inflation expectations have a stronger influence on businesses' wage- and price-setting decisions compared to professional forecaster expectations (Coibion and Gorodnichenko 2025).
The paper notes that workers expecting 4 percent inflation will demand salary increases to maintain purchasing power, creating a feedback loop to realized inflation.
why_it_matters: This research directly informs Federal Reserve policy considerations by identifying whether recent inflation expectation movements reflect normal reactions to price shocks or potentially destabilizing de-anchoring. If expectations are becoming unmooted from actual price data—as the March 2025 surge suggests—businesses may continue raising wages and prices even after the initial inflationary shocks subside, making the Fed's 2 percent target harder to achieve. Policymakers, investors, and businesses should monitor household expectations surveys as leading indicators of inflation persistence.
macro_context: The paper provides historical context for understanding the current inflation environment. The authors emphasize that de-anchored inflation expectations can become self-fulfilling: when households expect higher inflation, they demand higher wages, businesses raise prices, and actual inflation rises accordingly. This feedback mechanism was central to the Great Inflation of the 1970s and represents a key risk for current monetary policy. The Fed and other central banks closely monitor these measures to detect undue movements requiring policy response.
notes: Historical record: Published approximately 174 days prior to TODAY. Source is a high-quality Federal Reserve Bank research publication with clear macro catalysts relevance. The authors explicitly state views are their own and not official Federal Reserve System positions. The paper uses a straightforward regression methodology comparing salient price movements to survey data. Some truncation occurred in the source body, so full regression results or policy implications from the conclusion section may not be captured. Recommend human review to verify complete findings.
